name: Tishtrya (Star Deity of Rain)
shortDescription: Tishtrya is the Zoroastrian yazata (divine being) associated with Sirius, the brightest star, and is the deity of rain and agriculture. He is a powerful and benevolent figure who battles drought and brings life-giving waters to the land.
description: Tishtrya, in Zoroastrianism, is a yazata (divine being worthy of worship) whose primary role is that of a star deity, specifically associated with Sirius, the brightest star in the night sky. He is revered as the bringer of rain and the vanquisher of drought, playing a crucial role in ensuring agricultural prosperity and the continuation of life. The Avesta, the sacred texts of Zoroastrianism, detail Tishtrya's legendary battle against Apaosha, the demon of drought. This cosmic struggle represents the eternal conflict between good and evil, with Tishtrya embodying the forces of light and life, and Apaosha representing darkness and barrenness. In this epic confrontation, Tishtrya initially appears as a young man, but is weakened by the lack of proper offerings from humanity. He then transforms into a magnificent white stallion with golden ears, symbolizing his celestial power and purity. After a fierce three-day battle in the cosmic ocean, Tishtrya, strengthened by the prayers and sacrifices of the righteous, triumphs over Apaosha, releasing the life-giving waters and restoring fertility to the earth. This victory is celebrated as a symbol of hope and renewal, emphasizing the importance of devotion and righteousness in overcoming adversity. Tishtrya's influence extends beyond rainmaking; he is also considered a protector of travelers and a guardian against evil influences. His association with Sirius, a prominent celestial body, further reinforces his connection to the cosmos and his role as a divine benefactor. The Tir Yasht, a hymn dedicated to Tishtrya, provides detailed accounts of his exploits and underscores his significance in Zoroastrian cosmology and agricultural practices.

sources.title: The Zend-Avesta, Part III: The Yashts
sources.author: James Darmesteter
